`C:\Code\dterziev\CoreClaw\.superpowers\sdd\task-3-brief.md`

1	### Task 3: Per-repo migration procedure (repeat once per in-scope repo)
2	
3	This task is a **template applied to each in-scope repo from the audit**. With subagent-driven-development, dispatch **one subagent per repo**, passing the repo path, id, and default branch. Each repo's migration is independently reviewable and mergeable — that is the task boundary.
4	
5	**Files (in the target repo's working copy):**
6	- Create: `Directory.Packages.props`
7	- Modify: every `*.csproj` (strip `Version=` from `PackageReference`)
8	- Modify (if present): `Directory.Build.props` (move package-version properties into the props file)
9	
10	**Interfaces:**
11	- Consumes: from Task 2 — `{ path_with_namespace, id, default_branch, cpm_status }` for one repo.
12	- Produces: a row update in `projects/cpm-sweep/audit.md` (issue URL, MR URL, validation path, done checkbox), consumed by Task 4.
13	
14	- [ ] **Step 1: Clone the target repo and create the branch**
15	
16	```bash
17	cd <scratchpad>
18	glab repo clone <path_with_namespace> --  --depth 1 -b <default_branch>
19	cd "$(basename <path_with_namespace>)"
20	git checkout -b chore/adopt-cpm
21	```
22	
23	- [ ] **Step 2: Capture the baseline build (binlog)**
24	
25	Run: `dotnet restore && dotnet build -bl:<scratchpad>/<repo>-before.binlog -c Release`
26	Expected: either a clean build (record SUCCESS — this repo supports local validation) or a restore/build failure due to private feed / native deps / credentials (record CI-ONLY — skip local validation steps 6–8 and note it in the MR). If it fails for any *other* reason, stop and investigate before changing anything.
27	
28	- [ ] **Step 3: Run the convert-to-cpm skill**
29	
30	Invoke the `convert-to-cpm` skill on the repo's solution. It generates the root `Directory.Packages.props` (with `ManagePackageVersionsCentrally`=`true`), strips versions from `.csproj`, and inlines package-version properties from `Directory.Build.props`. Apply the consolidation policy from Global Constraints: **highest** version per package; any **major** jump becomes a per-project `VersionOverride` recorded for the MR body. For a `partial` repo, finish the conversion (enable MPVC, move residual versions) rather than starting fresh.
31	
32	- [ ] **Step 4: Inspect the generated props and overrides**
33	
34	Read `Directory.Packages.props`. Confirm: `ManagePackageVersionsCentrally` is `true`; one `<PackageVersion>` per package; no `<PackageReference>` in any `.csproj` still carries `Version=`; every `VersionOverride` corresponds to a real major-version conflict (list them for the MR).
35	
36	- [ ] **Step 5: Verify restore resolves identical versions**
37	
38	Run: `dotnet restore`
39	Expected: succeeds. (If the repo was CI-only at Step 2, this may still fail on auth — that's expected; defer to CI.)
40	
41	- [ ] **Step 6 (local-validatable repos only): Post-conversion build + binlog**
42	
43	Run: `dotnet build -bl:<scratchpad>/<repo>-after.binlog -c Release`
44	Expected: SUCCESS, matching the baseline. Compare resolved package versions between `<repo>-before.binlog` and `<repo>-after.binlog` (the `convert-to-cpm` skill's binlog comparison) — they must match except for intended consolidations.
45	
46	- [ ] **Step 7 (local-validatable repos only): Run the existing test suite**
47	
48	Run: `dotnet test -c Release`
49	Expected: PASS, or matches the pre-migration pass/fail baseline (record any pre-existing failures so they aren't attributed to this change).
50	
51	- [ ] **Step 7b: Patch layered-restore Dockerfiles (REQUIRED — systematic CI break)**
52	
53	CPM moves all package versions into `Directory.Packages.props`. Many data-services repos have a Dockerfile that does a **layered restore**: it `COPY`s the solution, each directory's `Directory.Build.props`, and all `*.csproj`, then runs `dotnet restore` **before** copying the full source (`COPY . ./`). Such a Dockerfile does NOT copy `Directory.Packages.props`, so after CPM the restore finds no versions and resolves every `PackageReference` to its **lowest** available version — the build then fails (e.g. `error : Invalid portable frameworks ... A hyphen may not be in any of the portable framework names`). Local build does NOT catch this; only CI does.
54	
55	Fix: inspect every Dockerfile in the repo (`build.Dockerfile`, `Dockerfile`, etc.). For each one that copies the root `Directory.Build.props` ahead of a `dotnet restore`, add immediately after that line:
56	
57	```dockerfile
58	COPY Directory.Packages.props ./
59	```
60	
61	Do NOT change test-stage Dockerfiles that build `FROM` the build image with `--no-restore` (they inherit the restore). If a Dockerfile restores via `COPY . ./` then `dotnet restore` (full source already present), no change is needed. If the repo has no Dockerfile, skip this step.
62	
63	- [ ] **Step 8: Commit**
64	
65	```bash
66	git add -A
67	git commit -m "chore: adopt Central Package Management"
68	```
69	
70	- [ ] **Step 9: Create the tracking issue on `Clarion/data`**
71	
72	Use the `glab` skill. Issue on `Clarion/data`, assignee `bhirani`, describing the *problem* (this repo has no / partial central package management; observed version drift), and *why* it's worth fixing. Capture the issue URL.
73	
74	```bash
75	glab issue create --repo Clarion/data \
76	  --title "Adopt Central Package Management in <repo name>" \
77	  --assignee bhirani \
78	  --description "<problem statement: no/partial CPM, version drift; why it matters. Reference the target repo as <path_with_namespace>#... with trailing +>"
79	```
80	
81	- [ ] **Step 10: Push and open the MR (ready, assigned, cross-linked)**
82	
83	Use the `glab` skill. Push `chore/adopt-cpm` and open an MR **in the target repo** against its default branch, `--remove-source-branch`, **not draft**, assigned to Bharat.
84	
85	```bash
86	glab mr create --repo <path_with_namespace> \
87	  --source-branch chore/adopt-cpm --target-branch <default_branch> \
88	  --remove-source-branch --assignee bhirani \
89	  --title "chore: adopt Central Package Management" \
90	  --description "<how this solves the issue: centralizes versions in Directory.Packages.props; consolidation summary; list of VersionOverrides + why; validation path used (local build+tests / CI-only); link the Clarion/data issue with trailing +>"
91	```
92	
93	Then add a note cross-linking the issue (and confirm the issue references the MR). Capture the MR URL.
94	
95	- [ ] **Step 11: Confirm the MR's CI build passes, then report**
96	
97	After opening the MR, find its pipeline and watch the **build/restore job** to confirm the Dockerfile patch worked (it must get past `dotnet restore` and build). Downstream test stages may fail for pre-existing reasons (no SQL/AWS creds in CI) — that's fine and not attributable to CPM; note it. If the build job fails because a layered-restore Dockerfile was missed in Step 7b, fix it, push, and re-check.
98	
99	Do **NOT** edit or commit anything in the CoreClaw repo (`c:/Code/dterziev/CoreClaw`). The controller owns `projects/cpm-sweep/audit.md`. Return your results (issue URL, MR URL, validation path, consolidation summary, Dockerfile-patched yes/no, CI build job status) to the controller, which updates the audit.
100	
101	---
102

Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\bhirani\.claude\plugins\cache\clarion-ai-skills\glab\1.5.0\skills\glab

# GitLab CLI (glab) Workflows

> **Script paths**: All script paths in this skill (e.g., `scripts/new_gitlab_workitem.py`) are relative to the skill's **base directory** shown at the top of the prompt. Always resolve them to absolute paths before executing. For example, if the base directory is `/home/user/.claude/plugins/cache/clarion-ai-skills/glab/1.0.0/skills/glab`, then run `python "/home/user/.claude/plugins/cache/clarion-ai-skills/glab/1.0.0/skills/glab/scripts/new_gitlab_workitem.py"`.

## Git Safety Rules (Apply to All Workflows)

These rules apply to every workflow below. Violations have caused real incidents; never ignore them.

- **Force-push only with `--force-with-lease`**, never plain `--force`. `--force-with-lease` fails if the remote has moved since your last fetch, protecting against clobbering teammates' commits.
- **Never force-push to `main` / `master`.** No exceptions — not even for hotfixes, not even with lease. If you think you need to, stop and ask.
- **Never push directly to `main` / `master`.** All changes go through MRs.
- **Pre-MR diff uses the remote target**, not local: `git diff origin/master...HEAD --stat`. Local `master` is often stale and will hide or add noise to the diff.

## Workflow: Push Changes (Default)

The most common action. Triggered by "push to gitlab", "push changes", "push to a new MR", "submit to gitlab", "create an MR", "create an issue and MR".

### Steps

1. **Check for existing MR** on the current branch and verify it is still open:

```bash
glab mr view -F json | jq '{state, title, description, web_url}'
```

- If the state is `opened`, continue to step 1a.
- If no MR exists, or the state is `merged` / `closed`, the branch is stale — **do not push to it**. Skip to step 2 to create a fresh branch + MR, even if the remote branch still exists.

1a. **Reconcile the MR description with current code.** Descriptions are written when the MR opens but drift as work continues — especially after review feedback. Read the current description (from step 1) and compare it against the branch as it stands:

```bash
TARGET=$(glab mr view -F json | jq -r '.target_branch')
git log $TARGET...HEAD --oneline
git diff $TARGET...HEAD --stat
```

Update the description if it no longer accurately describes **what the MR currently does and contains**. Some signals of drift:

- The summary describes intent or a plan that has since changed
- Listed changes are incomplete or name files/behaviour no longer present
- Test plan or checkboxes don't reflect the current test state
- Technical claims (approach, data flow, dependencies) don't match the code

If drift exists, update before pushing:

```bash
glab mr update --description "$(cat <<'EOF'
...rewritten to match current state...
EOF
)"
```

Preserve any CI-generated review branch URLs already in the description when rewriting — do not overwrite them with placeholders.

1b. **Now push:**

```bash
git add . && git commit -m "message"
git push
```

2. **Check project squash setting** (needed for MR creation):

```bash
glab api projects/:id | jq -r '.squash_option'
```

3. **Create issue and MR:**

Issues and MRs serve distinct purposes — keep them separate (see [Clarion Dev Standards: Issues and Merge Requests](https://gitlab.rivagecapital.com/Clarion/Clarion.Docs/-/blob/master/DevStandards.md#issues-and-merge-requests)):
- **Issue**: Describes the *problem*. Explains what is wrong or missing and *why* it is worth solving. Does not describe the solution.
- **MR**: Describes the *solution*. Explains how this change solves the problem stated in the issue.

Together they should give any reviewer full context: what the problem is, why it matters, and how the MR addresses it.

**Preferred:** Use the `new_gitlab_workitem.py` script which handles everything automatically:

```bash
python scripts/new_gitlab_workitem.py --title "Title here"
```

See [Helper Scripts](#helper-scripts) for full usage.

**Bash fallback:**

```bash
# Create issue — describe the problem and why it is worth solving
glab issue create -a @me -t "Issue title" -d "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem
What is wrong or missing? Describe the current state.

## Why
Why is this worth solving? What is the impact of not addressing it?
EOF
)" -y
# Note the issue number from output

# Create MR — describe the solution (add --squash-before-merge if squash_option is "always" or "default_on")
glab mr create -a @me -t "MR title" -d "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
How does this MR solve the problem described in the issue?

## Changes
- Change 1
- Change 2

Closes #<issue-number>
EOF
)" --push --remove-source-branch -y
```

**Note**: Always include `--remove-source-branch` when creating MRs via CLI. GitLab does not apply the project's default `remove_source_branch_after_merge` setting for API/CLI-created MRs.

4. **Display links** — Always show the Issue and MR links to the user after creation.

---

## Workflow: New Feature (Full Lifecycle)

Triggered by "work on a new feature", "start a new change", "create a branch".

### Steps

1. **Create branch from main:**

```bash
git fetch origin
git checkout -b feature/<name> origin/main --no-track
```

2. **Do the work** — make the requested code changes.

3. **Push changes** — follow [Workflow: Push Changes](#workflow-push-changes-default) to create issue + MR.

---

## Workflow: Review MR

Triggered by an MR link/URL, "review MR", "review this merge request", "check this MR".

### Steps

1. **Parse MR link** — Extract project path and MR IID from the URL:
   - Pattern: `https://gitlab.example.com/<group/project>/-/merge_requests/<IID>`
   - If no URL given (just MR number), assume current project.

2. **Read MR details:**

```bash
glab mr view <MR_IID>              # Overview
glab mr view <MR_IID> -c           # With comments
glab mr diff <MR_IID>              # Code diff
```

3. **Read discussion threads** (structured, with resolution status):

```bash
MR_IID=<IID>
glab api "projects/:id/merge_requests/$MR_IID/discussions" | jq '[.[] | select(.notes[0].resolvable) | {
  id: .id, resolved: .notes[0].resolved,
  surfer: (.notes[0].body | contains(":surfer:")),
  author: .notes[0].author.username,
  body: .notes[0].body[0:200]
}]'
```

4. **Checkout the MR branch** (if code fixes are needed):

```bash
glab mr checkout <MR_IID>
```

5. **Make fixes, commit, push.**

6. **Resolve discussions** after fixing:

```bash
glab api "projects/:id/merge_requests/$MR_IID/discussions/$DISCUSSION_ID" -X PUT -f resolved=true
```

---

## Workflow: Address Review Comments

Triggered by "check review comments", "address feedback", "address review comments".

### Steps

1. **View comments** (use the same discussion query as [Workflow: Review MR step 3](#workflow-review-mr)):

```bash
glab mr view -c
MR_IID=$(glab mr view -F json | jq -r '.iid')
glab api "projects/:id/merge_requests/$MR_IID/discussions" | jq '[.[] | select(.notes[0].resolvable) | {id: .id, resolved: .notes[0].resolved, surfer: (.notes[0].body | contains(":surfer:")), author: .notes[0].author.username, body: .notes[0].body[0:200]}]'
```

2. **Identify comment types and resolution ownership:**
   - **`:surfer:` comments** — non-blocking suggestions are indicated by inclusion of a surfer in the comment. Addressing these comments is optional and so the comment can be resolved directly by the MR author. If planning to address at a later point an issue should be created. The comment author is explicitly flagging "fine to resolve once you've addressed this". The MR author can resolve these after the fix lands.
   - **Other comments** — reply with explanation or make the change as needed. **Never resolve comments left by other users.** Only the comment author should resolve them; resolving someone else's thread prematurely closes the conversation they wanted to have.
   - Resolution state: in the `glab api .../discussions` output, `resolved: true` = resolved. **`resolved: undefined` or any other value means unresolved** — don't infer resolution from absence.
   - Always re-fetch discussions from the API before acting (`glab api .../discussions`); never rely on a cached view — comments change while you work.

3. **For each comment, fix the code**, then commit and push:

```bash
git add . && git commit -m "Address review feedback"
git push
```

4. **Resolve :surfer: discussions:**

```bash
glab api "projects/:id/merge_requests/$MR_IID/discussions/$DISCUSSION_ID" -X PUT -f resolved=true
```

5. **Reply to non-:surfer: discussions** (if needed):

```bash
glab api "projects/:id/merge_requests/$MR_IID/discussions/$DISCUSSION_ID/notes" -X POST -f body="Your reply here"
```

6. **Verify all resolved:**

```bash
MR_IID=$(glab mr view -F json | jq -r '.iid')
UNRESOLVED=$(glab api "projects/:id/merge_requests/$MR_IID/discussions" | jq '[.[] | select(.notes[0].resolvable and .notes[0].resolved == false)] | length')
echo "Unresolved discussions: $UNRESOLVED"
```

---

## Workflow: Merge

Triggered by "merge the MR", "merge this".

### Steps

1. **Mark ready for review** (if still draft):

```bash
glab mr update --ready
```

2. **Check mergeability:**

```bash
glab mr view -F json | jq '{mergeable: .merge_status, pipeline: .pipeline.status, approvals: .approvals_before_merge}'
```

3. **Merge:**

```bash
glab mr merge -s -d -y           # Squash, delete branch, no prompt
```

---

## Command Reference

For the full command reference (MR commands, issue commands, discussion API, bulk operations, API placeholders), see [references/command-reference.md](references/command-reference.md).

**Key**: `glab mr note` only adds standalone comments, not replies to discussion threads. Use the discussion API to reply to specific threads.

---

## Helper Scripts

### Create Issue and/or MR — new_gitlab_workitem.py

Use `new_gitlab_workitem.py` to create issues and MRs with a single command. The script automatically:
- Checks the project's `squash_option` and applies `--squash-before-merge` when appropriate
- Always includes `--remove-source-branch`
- Links the MR to the issue with `Closes #<issue-number>`
- Loads the project's description template when no `--description` / `--mr-description` is provided (see below)

**Description templates** — when no description is explicitly passed, the script loads the project's default template automatically. Sources are tried in order; the first match wins:
1. Local file `.gitlab/merge_request_templates/Default.md` (or `issue_templates/Default.md`) in the current working directory — only when `--repo` is not set (a local file belongs to the calling repo).
2. Project-level template field (`merge_requests_template` / `issues_template`) from the GitLab project's Settings → Merge requests / Issues — applies in both local and cross-repo mode.
3. Remote file `.gitlab/{folder}/Default.md` on the project's default branch, fetched via `glab api`.
4. Falls back to a minimal `## Summary\n\n## Changes\n` stub if no source yields a template.

When a template is loaded automatically, **you must fill in the template placeholders** with meaningful content based on the changes being made before passing it as `--mr-description` or `--description`. Do not pass a template with unfilled placeholders.

```bash
# Create both issue and MR
python scripts/new_gitlab_workitem.py --title "Add new feature"

# Create only an issue (description should explain the problem and why it is worth solving)
python scripts/new_gitlab_workitem.py --title "Bug report" --description "Details" --issue-only

# Create MR for existing issue (skip issue creation)
python scripts/new_gitlab_workitem.py --title "Add new feature" --issue-id 42

# Create MR with custom multiline description
python scripts/new_gitlab_workitem.py --title "Add feature" --mr-description "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
How this MR solves the problem described in the issue.

## Changes
- Change 1
- Change 2
EOF
)"

# Create a cross-repo issue with labels (e.g. a product ticket on another board)
python scripts/new_gitlab_workitem.py \
    --repo Clarion/product-public-api \
    --label "Clarion MCP" \
    --title "Per-user allowance overrides" \
    --description "Problem description here" \
    --issue-only
```

**Optional flags:**
- `--repo OWNER/REPO` — target a different project than the current git repo. Use this to raise product tickets on another board (e.g. `Clarion/product-public-api`) without leaving your working repo. Templates are still respected: the target project's `merge_requests_template` / `issues_template` settings and any `.gitlab/{folder}/Default.md` on its default branch are fetched automatically. Local `.gitlab/` files are not used cross-repo since they belong to the calling repo.
- `--label <label>` — apply a label to the issue and MR. Repeat the flag for multiple labels (e.g. `--label "Clarion MCP" --label bug`).

The script outputs JSON with `IssueUrl`, `IssueId`, `MRUrl`, `MRId`, and `SquashOption` on stdout for easy parsing. Progress messages go to stderr.

**IMPORTANT**: After running this script, always display the Issue and MR links to the user so they can quickly access them.

### Install (or update) glab (Windows)

If `glab` is missing on Windows, run the included installer script:

```powershell
powershell.exe -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File "scripts/install-glab-windows.ps1"
```

This installs the latest `glab.exe` in `%UserProfile%\.local\bin`, which should be in your PATH.

# Convert to Central Package Management

Migrate .NET projects from per-project package versioning to NuGet Central Package Management (CPM). CPM centralizes all package versions into a single `Directory.Packages.props` file, making version governance and upgrades easier across multi-project repositories.

## When to Use

- The user wants to adopt Central Package Management for a .NET repository, solution, or project
- Package versions are scattered across many `.csproj`, `.fsproj`, or `.vbproj` files and the user wants a single source of truth
- The user mentions `Directory.Packages.props`, CPM, or centralizing NuGet versions
- The user wants to update, bump, upgrade, align, or sync a NuGet package version across multiple projects -- CPM is the recommended approach for managing shared package versions, so suggest converting to CPM as part of the update if the projects use `PackageReference` and CPM is not already enabled
- Package versions are out of sync, conflicting, or mismatched across projects and the user wants to resolve or unify them

## When Not to Use

- The repository already has CPM fully enabled for all in-scope projects
- The user is working with `packages.config`-based projects (must first migrate to `PackageReference`)
- The user wants to manage versions via a custom MSBuild property file without using CPM

## Inputs

| Input | Required | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| Scope | Yes | A project file, solution file, or directory containing .NET projects to convert |
| Version conflict strategy | No | How to resolve cases where the same package has different versions across projects. When conflicts are detected, do not assume a default strategy -- ask the user which strategy to use or explicitly confirm a proposed strategy before proceeding. |

## Workflow

### Step 1: Determine scope

- **Single project**: User specifies a `.csproj`, `.fsproj`, or `.vbproj`.
- **Solution**: User specifies a `.sln` or `.slnx`. List projects with `dotnet sln list`.
- **Repository/directory**: No specific file given. Find all project files recursively from the first common ancestor directory of all .NET projects in scope.

If the scope is unclear, ask the user.

**Guard: Check for packages.config projects.** Before proceeding, check whether any project in scope uses `packages.config` instead of `PackageReference`. Look for `packages.config` files alongside project files. If any `packages.config` usage is detected, **stop and do not proceed with the conversion**. Inform the user that CPM requires projects with `PackageReference` format and that they must first migrate from `packages.config` to `PackageReference` (e.g., using Visual Studio's built-in migration or the `dotnet migrate` tooling). This skill cannot perform that migration.

### Step 2: Establish baseline build

Before making any changes, verify the scope builds successfully and capture a baseline binlog and package list. Run `dotnet clean`, then `dotnet build -bl:baseline.binlog`, then `dotnet package list --format json > baseline-packages.json`. Read [baseline-comparison.md](references/baseline-comparison.md) for the full procedure and fallback options. If the baseline build fails, stop and inform the user -- the scope must build cleanly before conversion. Do not delete `baseline.binlog` or `baseline-packages.json` -- they are needed for the post-conversion comparison and report.

### Step 3: Check for existing CPM

Search for any existing `Directory.Packages.props` in scope or ancestor directories. If CPM is already fully enabled, inform the user and stop. If a `Directory.Packages.props` exists without CPM enabled, ask whether to add the property to the existing file or create a new one.

### Step 4: Audit package references

Run `dotnet package list --format json` to get the resolved package references across all in-scope projects. Also scan `<Import>` elements to discover shared `.props`/`.targets` files containing package references.

Check for complexities: version conflicts, MSBuild property-based versions, conditional references, security advisories, and existing `VersionOverride` usage. Read [audit-complexities.md](references/audit-complexities.md) for the full checklist.

Present audit results to the user before proceeding, including:
- A table of each package, its version(s), and which projects use it
- Any version conflicts, security advisories, or complexities requiring decisions

When version conflicts exist, present each one individually with the affected projects, the distinct versions found, and the resolution options (align to highest, use `VersionOverride`, etc.) with their trade-offs. Do not upgrade any package beyond the highest version already in use across the scope -- this avoids introducing version incompatibilities or breaking changes that are unrelated to the CPM conversion itself. Note any known security advisories or other upgrade opportunities as follow-up items for the user to address after the conversion is complete. Ask the user to decide on each conflict before proceeding. Read [audit-complexities.md - Same package with different versions](references/audit-complexities.md) for the resolution workflow and presentation format.

### Step 5: Create or update Directory.Packages.props

Create the file with `dotnet new packagesprops` (.NET 8+) or manually. Add a `<PackageVersion>` entry for each unique package sorted alphabetically. For conditional versions or `VersionOverride` patterns, read [directory-packages-props.md](references/directory-packages-props.md).

### Step 6: Update project files

Remove the `Version` attribute from every `<PackageReference>` that now has a corresponding `<PackageVersion>`. Also update any shared `.props`/`.targets` files identified in step 4.

- Preserve all other attributes (`PrivateAssets`, `IncludeAssets`, `ExcludeAssets`, `GeneratePathProperty`, `Aliases`)
- Preserve conditional `<ItemGroup>` elements -- only remove the `Version` attribute within them
- Retain each file's existing indentation style (spaces vs. tabs, indentation depth) and blank lines -- do not reformat or reorganize unchanged lines
- Use `VersionOverride` (with user confirmation) when a project needs a different version than the central one

### Step 7: Handle MSBuild version properties

For `PackageReference` items that used MSBuild properties for versions, determine whether to inline the resolved value or keep the property reference in `Directory.Packages.props`. After validation succeeds in step 8, remove inlined version properties from `Directory.Build.props` or other files, verifying they have no remaining references. Read [msbuild-property-handling.md](references/msbuild-property-handling.md) for the decision workflow, import order requirements, and cleanup procedure.

### Step 8: Restore and validate

Run a clean restore and build, capturing a post-conversion binlog and package list. Run `dotnet clean`, then `dotnet build -bl:after-cpm.binlog`, then `dotnet package list --format json > after-cpm-packages.json`. Read [baseline-comparison.md](references/baseline-comparison.md) for the full procedure. If errors occur, read [validation-and-errors.md](references/validation-and-errors.md) for NuGet error codes and multi-TFM guidance.

**Do not delete or clean up any artifacts** (`baseline.binlog`, `after-cpm.binlog`, `baseline-packages.json`, `after-cpm-packages.json`). These files must be preserved for the user to inspect after the conversion. They are deliverables, not temporary files.

### Step 9: Post-conversion report

**You must create a `convert-to-cpm.md` file** alongside the binlog and JSON artifacts. Do not skip this step or substitute inline chat output for the file -- the user needs a persistent, shareable document. This file should be self-contained and shareable -- suitable for a pull request description, a team review, or a record of what was done. Structure the report with the following sections:

#### Section 1: Conversion overview

Summarize what was converted: the scope (project, solution, or repository), number of projects converted, total packages centralized, any projects or packages that were skipped, and any MSBuild properties that were inlined or removed. This gives the reader immediate context.

#### Section 2: Version conflict resolutions

If any version conflicts were encountered, list each one with:

- The package name and all versions that were found across projects
- Which projects used each version
- What the user decided (aligned to highest, used `VersionOverride`, etc.)
- The practical impact: which projects now resolve a different version than before, and which are unchanged

If no conflicts were found, state that all packages had consistent versions across projects -- this is a positive signal worth noting.

#### Section 3: Package comparison -- baseline vs. result

Compare `baseline-packages.json` and `after-cpm-packages.json` per project. See [baseline-comparison.md](references/baseline-comparison.md) for the comparison procedure. Present two tables:

- **Changes table**: Packages where the resolved version changed, a `VersionOverride` was introduced, or a package was added/removed. Include a status column explaining what changed and why (e.g., "VersionOverride -- project retains pinned version", "Aligned to highest version").
- **Unchanged table**: All other packages, confirming they resolve identically to baseline.

If there are no changes at all, state that the conversion is fully version-neutral -- this is the ideal outcome and provides reassurance.

#### Section 4: Risk assessment

Provide a clear confidence statement:

- **[Low risk]** -- Conversion is version-neutral; all packages resolve to the same versions as baseline. The build and restore succeeded. Recommend running `dotnet test` as a final check.
- **[Moderate risk]** -- Some packages changed versions (e.g., minor/patch alignment). List the affected packages and projects. Recommend reviewing the changes table and running `dotnet test` to verify no regressions.
- **[High risk]** -- Major version changes were applied, or packages were added/removed unexpectedly. Recommend careful review, running `dotnet test`, and comparing binlogs before merging.

Call out any specific warnings: `VersionOverride` usage that partially undermines centralization, or MSBuild property removal that could affect other build logic.

#### Section 5: Follow-up items

List any items identified during the conversion that the user should address separately after the CPM conversion is complete. These are intentionally out of scope for the conversion itself but important for the user to act on. Common follow-up items include:

- **Security advisories**: If any package versions are known to have security vulnerabilities (detected via `dotnet package list --vulnerable` or noted during the audit), list each advisory with the package name, current version, affected projects, and the minimum patched version. These upgrades are out of scope for the CPM conversion to avoid introducing version incompatibilities or breaking changes.
- **Deprecated packages**: If any packages are deprecated, note the recommended replacement.
- **Version alignment opportunities**: If `VersionOverride` was used to preserve differing versions, note that the user may want to align these in the future once the affected projects can be validated against the central version.
- **Test validation**: Recommend running `dotnet test` to validate runtime behavior beyond build success, especially if any version conflicts were resolved by aligning to the highest version.

Present follow-up items as a numbered checklist so the user can track them.

#### Section 6: Artifacts and how to use them

List the artifacts produced during conversion and explain how to use them:

- **`baseline.binlog`** and **`after-cpm.binlog`** -- MSBuild binary logs captured before and after conversion. These are available for manual validation and troubleshooting if needed.
- **`baseline-packages.json`** and **`after-cpm-packages.json`** -- Machine-readable snapshots of resolved package versions per project, used to produce the comparison tables above.
- **`convert-to-cpm.md`** -- This report file, suitable for use as a pull request description or team review artifact.

Recommend the user run `dotnet test` to validate runtime behavior beyond build success. If any version conflicts were resolved by aligning to the highest version, recommend reviewing the release notes for the affected packages.

## Validation

- [ ] Baseline build succeeded before any changes were made
- [ ] `Directory.Packages.props` exists with `ManagePackageVersionsCentrally` set to `true`
- [ ] Every in-scope `PackageReference` either has no `Version` attribute or uses `VersionOverride`
- [ ] Every referenced package has a corresponding `PackageVersion` entry
- [ ] `dotnet restore` and `dotnet build` complete without errors from a clean state
- [ ] Package list comparison shows no unexpected version changes
- [ ] No orphaned version properties remain (unless intentionally kept)

## More Info

- [Central Package Management documentation](https://github.com/NuGet/docs.microsoft.com-nuget/blob/main/docs/consume-packages/Central-Package-Management.md)
- [Validation and common errors](references/validation-and-errors.md)

## Problem

`data-services/coremont.snap.data.compare` has no Central Package Management (CPM) configured. Package versions are defined individually in each of the 11 `.csproj` files across the solution, leading to observed version drift:

- **Polly**: `8.5.2` in `DataComparer`, `7.2.3` in `WebApi` — a major-version split where the two projects use incompatible Polly major versions simultaneously.
- **Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk**: `17.3.2` in `Tests`/`IntegrationTests`, `16.11.0` in the `tests/` Lambda test projects — an inconsistency that can cause subtle test runner behaviour differences.
- **Moq**: `4.18.4` and `4.18.2` across test projects.
- **System.Data.SqlClient**: `4.8.6` and `4.8.5` across production projects.

Without a central version file, every upgrade requires touching multiple `.csproj` files, drift goes unnoticed at a glance, and version conflicts create risk of runtime mismatches in shared-dependency graphs.

## Why

Central Package Management (`Directory.Packages.props`) is the standard NuGet mechanism for enforcing a single source of truth for package versions across multi-project repositories. Adopting it:

- Makes version drift immediately visible (one file to audit).
- Ensures transitive dependency alignment is deliberate.
- Reduces the surface area for accidental version splits during upgrades.
- Is a required step for the data-services CPM sweep standardising all repos in the group.

Related MR: data-services/coremont.snap.data.compare#... (see MR for details)
EOF
